title: Welch Allyn Connex 6000 Series – Touchscreen Calibration Off / Dead Spots
description: Structured troubleshooting guide for touchscreen misalignment or dead spots on a Connex 6000 Series monitor.

steps.title: Check for Obstructions or Contamination
steps.instructions: Inspect the screen for dirt, dried disinfectant residue, moisture, or debris. Clean the screen using an approved disinfectant wipe. Allow the screen to fully dry before testing. Ensure no plastic film, tape, labels, or screen protectors are present.

steps.title: Confirm the Monitor Is Not Frozen
steps.instructions: Press multiple areas of the screen. Attempt to access different menus. Observe whether the display is updating normally or appears stuck. If the screen is frozen but powered on, proceed to a power cycle.

steps.title: Power Cycle the Monitor
steps.instructions: Turn the monitor off. Wait at least 10 seconds. Power the monitor back on. Allow the unit to complete a full boot sequence before testing touch response.

steps.title: Check for Visible Screen Damage
steps.instructions: Inspect for cracks, impact points, pressure marks, or discoloration. Lightly touch various areas and document where input is not detected. Note if touches register in the wrong location (misalignment).

steps.title: Observe and Document the Behavior Pattern
steps.instructions: Determine whether the issue is: Consistent dead zones in specific areas. Random intermittent non-response. Consistent touch offset (pressing one location activates another area). Complete non-response. Document any on-screen error messages or unusual behavior.

CCR.Complaint: “Touchscreen not responding correctly; dead spots on right side of screen.”
CCR.Cause: “Screen cleaned and dried. Unit power cycled. No visible cracks. Touch input remains unresponsive in upper-right quadrant. Suspected touchscreen digitizer failure.”
CCR.Resolution: “Removed from service, labeled Out of Service, sent for repair.” / “Tagged for Clinical Engineering bench evaluation.”

helpfulDetails: Final disposition (returned to service or sent for repair)

title: Welch Allyn Connex 6000 Series – NIBP Failure / Leaky Hose / Transducer Drift
description: Step-by-step guide for Clinical Engineering to troubleshoot NIBP errors caused by hose leaks or transducer drift on the Connex 6000 Series.

steps.title: Inspect the NIBP Hose
steps.instructions: Ensure the hose connecting the cuff to the monitor is fully seated at both ends. Examine the hose for cracks, holes, kinks, or wear. Replace the hose if any visible damage or leaks are detected. Even a small leak can prevent proper inflation and trigger an NIBP error.

steps.title: Check the Cuff and Connections
steps.instructions: Confirm the cuff is the correct size for the patient. Verify the cuff tubing is fully inserted and the hook/Velcro is secure. Inspect the bladder for tears or leaks. A poorly seated or damaged cuff can cause failed or inaccurate readings.

steps.title: Verify the Module / Transducer Seating
steps.instructions: Ensure the NIBP module is fully inserted in the monitor. Check connector pins for cleanliness, corrosion, or damage. Look for visible moisture or debris around the module. Loose or misaligned transducers can cause drift or persistent errors.

steps.title: Observe the Display
steps.instructions: Note any error codes (e.g., “NIBP Fail,” “Low Pressure,” “Leak Detected”). Record whether the error occurs immediately or intermittently. Documenting these details helps Clinical Engineering track recurring issues.

steps.title: Try a Simple Reset
steps.instructions: Remove the cuff and hose from the patient. Power cycle the monitor. Reconnect the hose and cuff, then attempt another measurement. If successful, the issue may have been a temporary connection or seating problem.

steps.title: If the Problem Persists
steps.instructions: If NIBP measurements still fail after completing the above steps, you have already checked the most common and easily verified causes: Hose integrity, Cuff correctness and placement, Module/transducer connections, Simple reset. The issue is now likely internal (transducer drift, module malfunction, or sensor calibration issue) and cannot be resolved at the bedside. Next Steps: Remove the monitor from clinical service. Clearly label it as Out of Service. Send it for repair or bench evaluation.

CCR.Cause: Hose inspected and intact, cuff verified, module seated correctly. Measurement still fails. Suspected transducer drift or internal module fault.
CCR.Resolution: Removed monitor from service, labeled Out of Service, sent for repair. Tagged for bench evaluation by CE.

helpfulDetails: Any visible damage, moisture, or debris
Final device disposition (returned to service or sent for repair)

title: Welch Allyn SureTemp Plus 692 Thermometer - Won’t Power On / Random Shutdowns
description: Guide to troubleshoot power-on failures and random shutdowns in the Welch Allyn SureTemp Plus 692 thermometer, focusing on external causes before assuming internal failure.

steps.title: Check Battery Condition
steps.instructions: Remove the battery from the thermometer. Inspect for corrosion, leakage, or swelling. Replace with a fully charged, compatible battery if needed.

steps.title: Inspect Battery Contacts
steps.instructions: Ensure the contacts inside the battery compartment are clean and free of debris. Lightly clean contacts with a dry cloth or a pencil eraser if corrosion is present.

steps.title: Test with a Known Good Battery
steps.instructions: Insert a new or verified fully charged battery. Power on the thermometer.

steps.title: Check Power Adapter (if using AC/charging)
steps.instructions: Verify the AC adapter is rated correctly for the device. Confirm outlet functionality by testing with another device. Inspect adapter and cable for fraying or damage.

steps.title: Inspect Thermometer Housing
steps.instructions: Check for signs of physical damage or moisture ingress. Ensure the unit is not excessively hot or cold.

steps.title: Reset the Device (if applicable)
steps.instructions: Some SureTemp 692 models allow a power reset by removing batteries for 1–2 minutes. Reinsert batteries and power on.

steps.title: Observe Operation
steps.instructions: Power on and monitor for stability. Note any pattern of shutdowns (immediately after powering on, during measurement, or randomly).

CCR.Complaint: Thermometer will not power on or randomly shuts down during use.
CCR.Cause: Battery contacts were corroded and original battery was weak.
CCR.Resolution: Cleaned battery contacts, replaced with a fully charged battery, device powers on consistently.

helpfulDetails: Presence of any corrosion or leakage
AC adapter voltage and outlet tested

title: Welch Allyn SureTemp Plus 692 Thermometer – Inaccurate Temperature Readings
description: Guide for troubleshooting inaccurate readings on Welch Allyn SureTemp Plus 692 thermometers, focusing on external causes before assuming internal failure.

steps.title: Verify Probe Type and Compatibility
steps.instructions: Ensure the correct probe is attached (oral, rectal, or axillary). Incompatible or damaged probes can produce inaccurate readings.

steps.title: Inspect Probe Covers
steps.instructions: Check that probe covers are intact and properly seated. A torn, loose, or reused cover can cause false readings.

steps.title: Check Device Calibration and Recent Verification
steps.instructions: Review the last calibration or verification date. If overdue, follow facility procedure for recalibration.

steps.title: Test on Known Reference Temperature
steps.instructions: Use a calibration check source (e.g., water bath or calibration simulator) to verify accuracy. This helps isolate whether the issue is the thermometer or technique.

steps.title: Check Battery / Power Status
steps.instructions: Low batteries can sometimes cause erratic readings. Replace batteries if low and retest.

steps.title: Examine Probe for Physical Damage or Contamination
steps.instructions: Look for cracks, debris, or dried fluid on the probe tip. Clean per manufacturer instructions; do not immerse in liquid beyond specifications.

steps.title: Ensure Proper Measurement Technique
steps.instructions: Follow correct placement and timing instructions per patient site. Patient movement or improper placement can skew readings.

CCR.Complaint: Thermometer giving readings 1–2°C higher than expected on multiple patients.
CCR.Cause: Probe covers intact; device cleaned; calibration overdue; low battery noted.
CCR.Resolution: Replaced batteries, verified readings against calibration source. Out-of-spec readings persisted; device removed from service and labeled OOS for repair.

helpfulDetails: Temperature readings compared to reference source
Final device status (removed, repaired, returned to service)

title: Welch Allyn SureTemp Plus 692 - Thermometer Won’t Take a Reading / Stuck on 'LO' or 'HI'
description: Guide to troubleshoot a SureTemp Plus 692 thermometer that fails to take readings or displays 'LO'/'HI'.

steps.title: Verify Correct Probe and Probe Cover
steps.instructions:
Ensure you are using a Welch Allyn-approved disposable probe cover on the appropriate probe. Using incorrect or no cover can cause inaccuracies or failed readings.
Confirm the correct probe type (oral/axillary or rectal) is installed for the intended measurement site.

steps.title: Check Probe Seating and Connection
steps.instructions:
Remove the probe from the probe well and then firmly reseat it until it clicks.
Inspect the probe cable and connector for damage, kinks, or corrosion.
A poorly seated or damaged probe often prevents valid readings.

steps.title: Inspect Ambient Conditions
steps.instructions: Make sure the thermometer and probe are within their operating temperature range. If the ambient temperature is too low or too high, the device may display an error instead of reading. Move the device into a normal room environment and allow it to stabilize for a few minutes.

steps.title: Battery Check
steps.instructions: Weak batteries can cause erratic behavior or failure to complete prediction cycles. Replace all three AA batteries with fresh alkaline batteries and retry.

steps.title: Try a New or Known‑Good Probe
steps.instructions: If available, swap in a different Welch Allyn probe and well assembly. Probe thermistors can fail or drift, leading to LO/HI readings; a good probe will isolate whether the probe assembly or the main unit is at fault.

steps.title: Confirm Proper Measurement Technique
steps.instructions:
Ensure the probe is in firm contact with the patient’s tissue and held steadily during the measurement cycle.
If taking axillary temperatures, the arm should be snug against the body to minimize ambient air exposure.

steps.title: Attempt a Cycle Reset
steps.instructions: Eject the probe from the well and then reinsert it. This can sometimes “reset” the measurement cycle and clear transient errors.

steps.title: Check for Device Security States
steps.instructions: Model 692 has security features that can disable operation (e.g., temperature count exhausted) showing messages rather than temperatures. Refer to the manual if SEC or other security indicators appear.

CCR.Complaint: User reports that the SureTemp Plus 692 displays “LO”/“HI” and fails to complete a temperature reading.
CCR.Cause: Verified poor probe contact, out‑of‑range ambient conditions, and low batteries were ruled out; error remained after trying a known‑good probe and fresh batteries.
CCR.Resolution: Unit removed from service, labeled Out of Service, and sent to authorized service center for repair.

title: Welch Allyn Connex 6000 Series – SpO₂ Probe Not Detected
description: Troubleshooting guide for situations where a Connex 6000 Series monitor reports that the SpO₂ probe is not detected.

steps.title: Confirm the Patient Is Not Actively Being Monitored
steps.instructions: Ensure the device is not currently connected to a patient for monitoring. If the device is in use, move the patient to another monitor before troubleshooting.

steps.title: Check the SpO₂ Probe Connection
steps.instructions: Inspect the SpO₂ probe connection at the monitor. Make sure the connector is fully seated in the SpO₂ port and not partially disconnected. Loose connectors are a common cause of probe detection errors.

steps.title: Verify the Correct Port Is Being Used
steps.instructions: Confirm that the probe is connected to the correct SpO₂ port on the monitor or SpO₂ module. Some monitors have multiple accessory ports, and incorrect placement may prevent detection.

steps.title: Inspect the Probe Cable and Connector
steps.instructions: Examine the probe cable for visible damage such as cuts, fraying, bent pins, or contamination. Damaged cables can interrupt communication between the probe and the monitor.

steps.title: Swap the SpO₂ Probe with a Known Good Probe
steps.instructions: Disconnect the current probe and connect a known working probe. If the new probe is detected, the original probe is likely faulty. If the probe is still not detected, the issue may involve the cable, module, or monitor port.

steps.title: Check the SpO₂ Extension Cable (If Present)
steps.instructions: Some setups include an extension cable between the monitor and probe. Remove the extension cable and connect the probe directly to the monitor. Extension cables are common failure points.

steps.title: Inspect for Contamination in the Port
steps.instructions: Look inside the SpO₂ port for debris, fluid intrusion, or bent contacts. Contamination can prevent proper electrical contact between the probe and the monitor.

steps.title: Reboot the Monitor
steps.instructions: Power cycle the monitor by turning it off, waiting several seconds, and turning it back on. Temporary software communication issues may clear after a reboot.

steps.title: Verify SpO₂ Module Recognition (If Modular Configuration)
steps.instructions: If the device uses a removable SpO₂ module, confirm that the module is properly seated and recognized by the monitor. A loose module connection may prevent probe detection.

steps.title: Test the Monitor with Multiple Probes
steps.instructions: Testing with two or more known-good probes can help confirm whether the issue is accessory-related or related to the monitor hardware.

CCR.Complaint: Nursing reported that the Connex 6000 monitor displays 'SpO₂ probe not detected' and no oxygen saturation values appear on the screen.
CCR.Cause: Damaged SpO₂ probe cable found during inspection. Monitor successfully detected a known-good probe.
CCR.Resolution: Replaced faulty SpO₂ probe with a working unit and verified SpO₂ waveform and numeric values returned to normal operation.

helpfulDetails: Any contamination found in the port
Final operational status of SpO₂ measurement

title: Welch Allyn Connex 6000 Series – Temperature Probe Error
description: Troubleshooting a temperature probe error on the Welch Allyn Connex 6000 Series vital signs monitor.

steps.title: Ensure Patient Safety First
steps.instructions: Verify the device is not actively being used on a patient. If the monitor is in use, move the patient to another monitor before beginning troubleshooting. This ensures patient care continues without interruption while the device is evaluated.

steps.title: Confirm the error message displayed on the monitor
steps.instructions: Verify the monitor specifically indicates a temperature probe error. This helps confirm the issue is related to the probe or module rather than a general system fault.

steps.title: Inspect the temperature probe connection
steps.instructions: Ensure the probe is fully seated in the temperature port on the side of the monitor. Loose or partially connected probes are a common cause of detection errors.

steps.title: Check for visible damage to the probe and cable
steps.instructions: Look for frayed cables, bent connectors, cracked housings, or exposed wiring. Damaged probes can trigger errors or prevent the monitor from detecting the probe.

steps.title: Disconnect and reconnect the probe
steps.instructions: Remove the probe from the monitor and reconnect it firmly. This can correct intermittent connection issues caused by contact oxidation or incomplete insertion.

steps.title: Verify the correct probe type is being used
steps.instructions: Ensure the probe is compatible with the Connex 6000 Series monitor and matches the temperature module installed (for example, oral/axillary or rectal probe types). Incorrect probes may not be recognized.

steps.title: Check the probe well and connector port
steps.instructions: Inspect the temperature probe well and port for debris, bent pins, fluid contamination, or physical damage that could interfere with the connection.

steps.title: Swap with a known-good temperature probe
steps.instructions: Connect a probe that is known to work properly on another Connex monitor. If the new probe works, the original probe is likely faulty.

steps.title: Power cycle the monitor
steps.instructions: Turn the monitor off, wait approximately 10 seconds, and power it back on. This resets the device and may clear temporary communication faults.

steps.title: Observe monitor behavior during a temperature attempt
steps.instructions: Attempt a temperature reading and watch for any alarms, error codes, or unusual behavior that may indicate whether the issue is probe-related or internal.

CCR.Complaint: Nursing reported the Connex 6000 monitor displays a temperature probe error and will not take a temperature reading.
CCR.Cause: Temperature probe cable found damaged near the connector. Monitor displayed probe error when connected.
CCR.Resolution: Replaced faulty temperature probe with known-good probe. Verified temperature measurement functions correctly.

title: Welch Allyn Connex 6000 Series – Battery Not Charging
description: Troubleshooting guide for situations where the Connex 6000 Series vital signs monitor battery does not charge.

steps.title: Ensure Patient Safety First
steps.instructions: Confirm the monitor is not actively being used on a patient before beginning troubleshooting. If the device cannot maintain battery power, move the patient to another monitor before removing the device from service.

steps.title: Verify the device is connected to AC power
steps.instructions: Confirm the monitor is plugged into a known-good hospital outlet or charging dock. If the device is not receiving AC power, the battery will not charge. Look for power indicator lights, the charging icon on the display, and docking station indicators if used. If the device powers off when unplugged, the battery may be depleted or failed.

steps.title: Check the AC power adapter and power cord
steps.instructions: Inspect the power supply and cord for loose connections, frayed or damaged cable, or bent pins at the connector. If available, test with a known-good power adapter compatible with the Connex 6000 Series. A faulty power adapter is a common cause of charging failure.

steps.title: Inspect the docking station (if used)
steps.instructions: Many Connex 6000 monitors charge through a wall mount or rolling stand dock. Check for loose dock connection, bent or dirty charging contacts, or debris on docking contacts. Remove the monitor from the dock and reconnect it firmly to ensure proper contact. If possible, test charging using the direct AC adapter instead of the dock.

steps.title: Check battery charge status in the system menu
steps.instructions: Navigate to the device settings or battery status screen to confirm battery presence detected, charging status, and battery health information if available. If the monitor does not detect the battery, the battery may be improperly seated or failed.

steps.title: Power cycle the monitor
steps.instructions: A system restart may reset battery management functions. Turn the monitor off, disconnect AC power, wait 30 seconds, reconnect power and restart the device, then observe whether the charging indicator appears after reboot.

steps.title: Inspect battery compartment (external check only)
steps.instructions: If accessible without disassembly, verify the battery is properly installed, ensure the battery latch is secure, and check for corrosion or debris on contacts. If the battery appears swollen, damaged, or hot, remove the device from service immediately.

steps.title: Test with a known-good battery if available
steps.instructions: If a spare battery is available, install a known-good battery, connect the monitor to AC power, and observe whether charging begins. If the replacement battery charges normally, the original battery is likely at end-of-life.

CCR.Complaint: Nursing reported the Connex 6000 monitor battery was not charging and the device shut off when unplugged.
CCR.Cause: Battery remained at 0% despite being connected to AC power. Tested with known-good power adapter and dock with same result. Battery likely failed.
CCR.Resolution: Removed device from service and replaced battery. Verified charging indicator and confirmed battery level increased while connected to AC power.

helpfulDetails: Final device status (returned to service or sent for repair)

title: Welch Allyn Connex 6000 Series - Touchscreen Unresponsive
description: Guide for troubleshooting an unresponsive touchscreen on the Connex 6000 Series monitor, emphasizing external causes and proper escalation.

steps.title: Confirm the device is powered on and not in sleep mode
steps.instructions: Ensure the monitor is powered on and the display is active. Press the power button once to wake the screen if necessary. A dark or sleeping display can appear unresponsive.

steps.title: Verify the device is not actively monitoring a patient before troubleshooting
steps.instructions: Ensure the device is not in use on a patient. Move the patient to another monitor if necessary before proceeding.

steps.title: Inspect the touchscreen surface
steps.instructions: Check the screen for fluids, gel, dirt, or debris. Dried cleaning solution, ultrasound gel, or bodily fluids can interfere with capacitive touch detection. Clean the screen with an approved hospital disinfectant wipe and dry it with a lint-free cloth.

steps.title: Remove gloves and test touch response
steps.instructions: Some thick or specialty gloves can reduce touchscreen sensitivity. Test the screen briefly with a bare finger to determine if the issue is glove-related.

steps.title: Check for protective covers or screen protectors
steps.instructions: Verify that no aftermarket screen protectors, plastic covers, or tape are interfering with the touchscreen. Remove anything covering the display and test again.

steps.title: Inspect for physical damage
steps.instructions: Check the display for cracks, pressure marks, or liquid intrusion. Physical damage can cause partial or complete touchscreen failure.

steps.title: Reboot the monitor
steps.instructions: Perform a controlled reboot by pressing and holding the power button until the device powers down, then power it back on. Many touchscreen issues are caused by temporary software lockups.

steps.title: Verify system responsiveness during reboot
steps.instructions: Observe whether the screen responds during the boot process. If the display shows normal startup behavior but becomes unresponsive afterward, the issue may be software related.

steps.title: Disconnect external accessories
steps.instructions: Remove USB devices, barcode scanners, or other accessories that may be connected. Occasionally peripheral communication issues can cause interface delays or freezing.

steps.title: Test multiple screen areas
steps.instructions: Tap different areas of the screen to determine whether the issue affects the entire touchscreen or only a specific region. Partial failures often indicate digitizer damage.

steps.title: Check AC power and battery condition
steps.instructions: Ensure the monitor is properly connected to AC power and the battery is not critically low. Low voltage conditions can sometimes cause unstable behavior.

CCR.Complaint: Nursing reported the Connex 6000 touchscreen was not responding to touch inputs and they were unable to enter patient information.
CCR.Cause: Touchscreen was completely unresponsive despite normal display operation. Cleaning, accessory removal, and device reboot did not restore functionality.
CCR.Resolution: Device removed from service and labeled Out of Service. Unit sent to Clinical Engineering shop for display/touchscreen evaluation and potential replacement.

helpfulDetails: Touch response in different screen areas
Final device status (removed from service or returned to use)
